([/ˈʃreɪdər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); born July 22, 1946) is an American screenwriter, film director, and film critic. He first became known for writing the screenplay of [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese")'s *[Taxi Driver](/wiki/Taxi_Driver "Taxi Driver")* (1976). He later continued his collaboration with Scorsese, writing or co-writing *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")* (1980), *[The Last Temptation of Christ](/wiki/The_Last_Temptation_of_Christ_(film) "The Last Temptation of Christ (film)")* (1988), and *[Bringing Out the Dead](/wiki/Bringing_Out_the_Dead "Bringing Out the Dead")* (1999). Schrader has also worked extensively as a director: his 23 films include *[Blue Collar](/wiki/Blue_Collar_(film) "Blue Collar (film)")* (1978), *[Hardcore](/wiki/Hardcore_(1979_film) "Hardcore (1979 film)")* (1979), *[American Gigolo](/wiki/American_Gigolo "American Gigolo")* (1980), *[Mishima: A Life in Four Chapters](/wiki/Mishima:_A_Life_in_Four_Chapters "Mishima: A Life in Four Chapters")* (1985), *[Light Sleeper](/wiki/Light_Sleeper "Light Sleeper")* (1992), *[Affliction](/wiki/Affliction_(1997_film) "Affliction (1997 film)")* (1997), and *[First Reformed](/wiki/First_Reformed "First Reformed")* (2017), with the last of these earning him his first [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Original_Screenplay "Academy Award for Best Original Screenplay") nomination. Schrader's work frequently depicts "man in a room" stories which feature isolated, troubled men confronting an [existential crisis](/wiki/Existential_crisis "Existential crisis").[[1]](#cite_note-bfi-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

American film director (born 1946)

For the United Auto Workers activist, see [Paul Schrade](/wiki/Paul_Schrade "Paul Schrade").

**Paul Joseph Schrader** ([/ˈʃreɪdər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); born July 22, 1946) is an American screenwriter, film director, and film critic. He first became known for writing the screenplay of [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese")'s *[Taxi Driver](/wiki/Taxi_Driver "Taxi Driver")* (1976). He later continued his collaboration with Scorsese, writing or co-writing *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")* (1980), *[The Last Temptation of Christ](/wiki/The_Last_Temptation_of_Christ_(film) "The Last Temptation of Christ (film)")* (1988), and *[Bringing Out the Dead](/wiki/Bringing_Out_the_Dead "Bringing Out the Dead")* (1999). Schrader has also worked extensively as a director: his 23 films include *[Blue Collar](/wiki/Blue_Collar_(film) "Blue Collar (film)")* (1978), *[Hardcore](/wiki/Hardcore_(1979_film) "Hardcore (1979 film)")* (1979), *[American Gigolo](/wiki/American_Gigolo "American Gigolo")* (1980), *[Mishima: A Life in Four Chapters](/wiki/Mishima:_A_Life_in_Four_Chapters "Mishima: A Life in Four Chapters")* (1985), *[Light Sleeper](/wiki/Light_Sleeper "Light Sleeper")* (1992), *[Affliction](/wiki/Affliction_(1997_film) "Affliction (1997 film)")* (1997), and *[First Reformed](/wiki/First_Reformed "First Reformed")* (2017), with the last of these earning him his first [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Original_Screenplay "Academy Award for Best Original Screenplay") nomination. Schrader's work frequently depicts "man in a room" stories which feature isolated, troubled men confronting an [existential crisis](/wiki/Existential_crisis "Existential crisis").[[1]](#cite_note-bfi-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Raised in a strict [Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinist "Calvinist") family, Schrader attended [Calvin College](/wiki/Calvin_College "Calvin College") before pursuing [film studies](/wiki/Film_studies "Film studies") at [UCLA](/wiki/UCLA "UCLA") on the encouragement of film critic [Pauline Kael](/wiki/Pauline_Kael "Pauline Kael"). He then worked as a film scholar and critic, publishing the book *[Transcendental Style in Film: Ozu, Bresson, Dreyer](/wiki/Transcendental_Style_in_Film "Transcendental Style in Film")* (1972) before transitioning to screenwriting in 1974. The success of *Taxi Driver* in 1976 brought greater attention to his work, and Schrader began directing his own films, beginning with *Blue Collar* (co-written with his brother, [Leonard Schrader](/wiki/Leonard_Schrader "Leonard Schrader")). Schrader has described three of his recent films as a loose trilogy: *[First Reformed](/wiki/First_Reformed "First Reformed")* (2017), *[The Card Counter](/wiki/The_Card_Counter "The Card Counter")* (2021), and *[Master Gardener](/wiki/Master_Gardener_(film) "Master Gardener (film)")* (2022).

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Schrader&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Schrader was born in [Grand Rapids, Michigan](/wiki/Grand_Rapids,_Michigan "Grand Rapids, Michigan"), the son of Joan (*née* Fisher) and Charles A. Schrader, an executive.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Schrader's family attended the [Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism") [Christian Reformed Church](/wiki/Christian_Reformed_Church_in_North_America "Christian Reformed Church in North America").[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) Schrader's mother was of Dutch descent, the daughter of emigrants from [Friesland](/wiki/Friesland "Friesland"), while Schrader's paternal grandfather was from a German family that had come to the US through Canada.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

His early life was based upon the religion's strict principles and parental education. He did not see a film until he was seventeen years old when he was able to sneak away from home. In an interview, he stated that *[The Absent-Minded Professor](/wiki/The_Absent-Minded_Professor "The Absent-Minded Professor")* was the first film he saw. In his own words, he was "very unimpressed" by it, while *[Wild in the Country](/wiki/Wild_in_the_Country "Wild in the Country")*, which he saw sometime later, had quite some effect on him.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Schrader attributes his intellectual rather than emotional approach towards movies and movie-making to his having no adolescent movie memories.[[8]](#cite_note-sos-8)

Schrader earned his B.A. in philosophy with a minor in theology from [Calvin College](/wiki/Calvin_College "Calvin College") but decided against becoming a minister.[[9]](#cite_note-wsj-9) He then earned an [M.A.](/wiki/Master_of_Arts "Master of Arts") in [film studies](/wiki/Film_studies "Film studies") at the [UCLA Film School](/wiki/UCLA_Film_School "UCLA Film School") upon the recommendation of [Pauline Kael](/wiki/Pauline_Kael "Pauline Kael"), who encouraged him to be a film critic.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Schrader first became a [film critic](/wiki/Film_critic "Film critic"), writing for the *[Los Angeles Free Press](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Free_Press "Los Angeles Free Press")* and later for *Cinema* magazine. His book *[Transcendental Style in Film: Ozu, Bresson, Dreyer](/wiki/Transcendental_Style_in_Film "Transcendental Style in Film")*, which examines the similarities between [Robert Bresson](/wiki/Robert_Bresson "Robert Bresson"), [Yasujirō Ozu](/wiki/Yasujir%C5%8D_Ozu "Yasujirō Ozu"), and [Carl Theodor Dreyer](/wiki/Carl_Theodor_Dreyer "Carl Theodor Dreyer"), was published in 1972. Other film-makers who made a lasting impression on Schrader are [John Ford](/wiki/John_Ford "John Ford"), [Jean Renoir](/wiki/Jean_Renoir "Jean Renoir"), [Roberto Rossellini](/wiki/Roberto_Rossellini "Roberto Rossellini"), [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock"), and [Sam Peckinpah](/wiki/Sam_Peckinpah "Sam Peckinpah"). Renoir's *[The Rules of the Game](/wiki/The_Rules_of_the_Game "The Rules of the Game")* he called the "quintessential movie" which represents "all of the cinema".[[8]](#cite_note-sos-8)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Schrader&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### 1974–1989: Screenwriter and directorial debut

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Schrader&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1974–1989: Screenwriter and directorial debut")]

In 1974, Schrader and his brother [Leonard](/wiki/Leonard_Schrader "Leonard Schrader") co-wrote *[The Yakuza](/wiki/The_Yakuza "The Yakuza")*, a film set in the Japanese crime world. The script became the subject of a bidding war, eventually selling for $325,000. The film was directed by [Sydney Pollack](/wiki/Sydney_Pollack "Sydney Pollack") and starred [Robert Mitchum](/wiki/Robert_Mitchum "Robert Mitchum"). [Robert Towne](/wiki/Robert_Towne "Robert Towne"), best known for *[Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown_(1974_film) "Chinatown (1974 film)")*, also received a credit for his rewrite. Although *The Yakuza* failed commercially, it brought Schrader to the attention of the [new generation](/wiki/New_Hollywood "New Hollywood") of [Hollywood](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_States "Cinema of the United States") directors. In 1975, he wrote the script for *[Obsession](/wiki/Obsession_(1976_film) "Obsession (1976 film)")* for [Brian De Palma](/wiki/Brian_De_Palma "Brian De Palma"). Schrader wrote an early draft of [Steven Spielberg](/wiki/Steven_Spielberg "Steven Spielberg")'s *[Close Encounters of the Third Kind](/wiki/Close_Encounters_of_the_Third_Kind "Close Encounters of the Third Kind")* (1977), but Spielberg disliked the script, calling it "terribly guilt-ridden", and opted for something lighter.[[11]](#cite_note-Spielberg-11) He also wrote an early draft of *[Rolling Thunder](/wiki/Rolling_Thunder_(film) "Rolling Thunder (film)")* (1977), which the film's producers reworked without his participation. He disapproved of the final film.[[8]](#cite_note-sos-8) Schrader's script about an obsessed New York City taxi driver became Martin Scorsese's film *[Taxi Driver](/wiki/Taxi_Driver "Taxi Driver")*, which was nominated for the [Oscar](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") for [Best Picture](/wiki/Best_Picture "Best Picture") and won the [Palme d'Or](/wiki/Palme_d%27Or "Palme d'Or") at the [Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/Cannes_Film_Festival "Cannes Film Festival"). Besides *Taxi Driver* (1976), Scorsese also drew on scripts by Schrader for *Raging Bull* (1980), co-credited to [Mardik Martin](/wiki/Mardik_Martin "Mardik Martin"); *[The Last Temptation of Christ](/wiki/The_Last_Temptation_of_Christ_(film) "The Last Temptation of Christ (film)")* (1988); and *[Bringing Out the Dead](/wiki/Bringing_Out_the_Dead "Bringing Out the Dead")* (1999).

Thanks partly to critical acclaim for *Taxi Driver*, Schrader was able to direct his first feature, *[Blue Collar](/wiki/Blue_Collar_(film) "Blue Collar (film)")* (1978), co-written with his brother Leonard. *Blue Collar* features [Richard Pryor](/wiki/Richard_Pryor "Richard Pryor"), [Harvey Keitel](/wiki/Harvey_Keitel "Harvey Keitel"), and [Yaphet Kotto](/wiki/Yaphet_Kotto "Yaphet Kotto") as car factory workers attempting to escape their socio-economic rut through theft and [blackmail](/wiki/Blackmail "Blackmail"). He has described the film as challenging to make, because of the artistic and personal tensions between him and the cast. During principal photography, he suffered an on-set mental collapse, which led him to reconsider his career seriously. [John Milius](/wiki/John_Milius "John Milius") acted as executive producer on the following year's *[Hardcore](/wiki/Hardcore_(1979_film) "Hardcore (1979 film)"),* again written by Schrader, a film with many autobiographical parallels in his depiction of the Calvinist milieu of Grand Rapids, and in the character of [George C. Scott](/wiki/George_C._Scott "George C. Scott"), which was based on Schrader's father.[[8]](#cite_note-sos-8) Among Paul Schrader's films in the 1980s were *[American Gigolo](/wiki/American_Gigolo "American Gigolo")* starring [Richard Gere](/wiki/Richard_Gere "Richard Gere") (1980), his *[Cat People](/wiki/Cat_People_(1982_film) "Cat People (1982 film)")* (1982) a remake of the 1942 film *[Cat People](/wiki/Cat_People_(1942_film) "Cat People (1942 film)")*, and *[Mishima: A Life in Four Chapters](/wiki/Mishima:_A_Life_in_Four_Chapters "Mishima: A Life in Four Chapters")* (1985). Inspired by Japanese writer [Yukio Mishima](/wiki/Yukio_Mishima "Yukio Mishima"), the film interweaves episodes from Mishima's life with dramatizations of segments from his books. *Mishima* was nominated for the top prize (the [Palme d'Or](/wiki/Palme_d%27Or "Palme d'Or")) at the [Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/1985_Cannes_Film_Festival "1985 Cannes Film Festival"). [Francis Ford Coppola](/wiki/Francis_Ford_Coppola "Francis Ford Coppola") and [George Lucas](/wiki/George_Lucas "George Lucas") served as executive producers. Schrader also directed *[Patty Hearst](/wiki/Patty_Hearst_(film) "Patty Hearst (film)")* (1988), about the kidnapping and transformation of the [Hearst Corporation heiress](/wiki/Patty_Hearst "Patty Hearst"). In 1987, he was a member of the jury at the [37th Berlin International Film Festival](/wiki/37th_Berlin_International_Film_Festival "37th Berlin International Film Festival").[[12]](#cite_note-Berlinale-12)

### 1990–2016: Career fluctuations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Schrader&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1990–2016: Career fluctuations")]

His 1990s work included the travelers-in-Venice tale *[The Comfort of Strangers](/wiki/The_Comfort_of_Strangers_(film) "The Comfort of Strangers (film)")* (1990), adapted by [Harold Pinter](/wiki/Harold_Pinter "Harold Pinter") from the [Ian McEwan](/wiki/Ian_McEwan "Ian McEwan") novel, and *[Light Sleeper](/wiki/Light_Sleeper "Light Sleeper")* (1992), a sympathetic study of a drug dealer vying for a normal life. In 2005, Schrader described *Light Sleeper* as his "most personal" film.[[13]](#cite_note-13) In 1997, he made *[Touch](/wiki/Touch_(1997_film) "Touch (1997 film)")* (1997), based on an [Elmore Leonard](/wiki/Elmore_Leonard "Elmore Leonard") novel about a young man seemingly able to cure the sick by the laying on of hands. In 1998, Schrader won critical acclaim for the drama *[Affliction](/wiki/Affliction_(1997_film) "Affliction (1997 film)")*. The film tells the story of a troubled small-town policeman ([Nick Nolte](/wiki/Nick_Nolte "Nick Nolte")) who becomes obsessed with solving the mystery behind a fatal hunting accident. Schrader's script was based on the novel by [Russell Banks](/wiki/Russell_Banks "Russell Banks"). The film was nominated for multiple awards, including two Academy Awards for acting (for Nolte and [James Coburn](/wiki/James_Coburn "James Coburn")). Schrader received the [Austin Film Festival's](/wiki/Austin_Film_Festival "Austin Film Festival") Distinguished Screenwriter Award the same year. In 1999, Schrader received the [Laurel Award for Screenwriting Achievement](/wiki/Laurel_Award_for_Screenwriting_Achievement "Laurel Award for Screenwriting Achievement") from the [Writers Guild of America](/wiki/Writers_Guild_of_America "Writers Guild of America").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Paul_Schrader.jpg/250px-Paul_Schrader.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paul_Schrader.jpg)

Schrader at the [Karlovy Vary International Film Festival](/wiki/Karlovy_Vary_International_Film_Festival "Karlovy Vary International Film Festival") 2009

In 2002, he directed the well received [biopic](/wiki/Biopic "Biopic") *[Auto Focus](/wiki/Auto_Focus "Auto Focus")*, based on the life and murder of *[Hogan's Heroes](/wiki/Hogan%27s_Heroes "Hogan's Heroes")* actor [Bob Crane](/wiki/Bob_Crane "Bob Crane"). In 2003, Schrader made entertainment headlines after being fired from *The Exorcist: Dominion*, a prequel film to the horror classic *[The Exorcist](/wiki/The_Exorcist "The Exorcist")* from 1973. The film's production companies [Morgan Creek Productions](/wiki/Morgan_Creek_Productions "Morgan Creek Productions") and [Warner Bros. Pictures](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Pictures "Warner Bros. Pictures") intensely disliked the film Schrader had made. Director [Renny Harlin](/wiki/Renny_Harlin "Renny Harlin") was hired to re-shoot nearly the entire movie, which was released as *[Exorcist: The Beginning](/wiki/Exorcist:_The_Beginning "Exorcist: The Beginning")* on August 20, 2004, to disastrously negative reviews and embarrassing box office receipts. Warner Bros. and Morgan Creek put over $80 million into the endeavor, and Harlin's film only made back $41 million domestically. Schrader's version of the film eventually premiered at the [Brussels International Festival of Fantastic Film](/wiki/Brussels_International_Festival_of_Fantastic_Film "Brussels International Festival of Fantastic Film") on March 18, 2005, as *Exorcist: The Original Prequel*. Due to extreme interest in Schrader's version from critics and cinephiles alike, Warner Bros. agreed to give the film a limited theatrical release later that year under the title *[Dominion: Prequel to the Exorcist](/wiki/Dominion:_Prequel_to_the_Exorcist "Dominion: Prequel to the Exorcist")*. The film was only shown on 110 screens around the United States and made just $251,000. The critics liked Schrader's version much better than Harlin's. However, Schrader's film ultimately met with a generally negative reaction.

After that, Schrader filmed *[The Walker](/wiki/The_Walker "The Walker")* (2007), starring [Woody Harrelson](/wiki/Woody_Harrelson "Woody Harrelson") as a male escort caught up in a political murder enquiry, and the Israel-set *[Adam Resurrected](/wiki/Adam_Resurrected_(film) "Adam Resurrected (film)")* (2008), which stars [Jeff Goldblum](/wiki/Jeff_Goldblum "Jeff Goldblum") and [Willem Dafoe](/wiki/Willem_Dafoe "Willem Dafoe"). Schrader headed the International Jury of the 2007 Berlin International Film Festival and in 2011 became a jury member for the ongoing [Filmaka](/wiki/Filmaka "Filmaka") short film contest.[[14]](#cite_note-Filmaka-14) On July 2, 2009, Schrader was awarded the inaugural Lifetime Achievement in Screenwriting award at the ScreenLit Festival in [Nottingham](/wiki/Nottingham "Nottingham"), [England](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). Several of his films were shown at the festival, including *Mishima: A Life in Four Chapters*, which followed the presentation of the award by director [Shane Meadows](/wiki/Shane_Meadows "Shane Meadows").

After five years of trying and failing to find funding to make feature films, Schrader returned with *[The Canyons](/wiki/The_Canyons_(film) "The Canyons (film)")* (2013), an erotic dramatic thriller written by [Bret Easton Ellis](/wiki/Bret_Easton_Ellis "Bret Easton Ellis") and starring [Lindsay Lohan](/wiki/Lindsay_Lohan "Lindsay Lohan") and adult-film star [James Deen](/wiki/James_Deen "James Deen"). The film was one of the first films to use the website [Kickstarter](/wiki/Kickstarter "Kickstarter") to crowd-source its funding. Schrader also used the website Let It Cast to have unknown actors submit their audition tapes over the internet. [American Apparel](/wiki/American_Apparel "American Apparel") provided some wardrobe for the film. The film was ultimately made for just $250,000 and had a limited theatrical release from [IFC Films](/wiki/IFC_Films "IFC Films") on August 2, 2013. The film was poorly received by general critics and audiences. The film only made $56,000 in theaters but found later success when released on various [Video on Demand](/wiki/Video_on_Demand "Video on Demand") platforms.

In 2014, Schrader directed *[The Dying of the Light](/wiki/Dying_of_the_Light_(film) "Dying of the Light (film)")*, an espionage thriller starring [Nicolas Cage](/wiki/Nicolas_Cage "Nicolas Cage") as a government agent suffering from a deadly disease, [Anton Yelchin](/wiki/Anton_Yelchin "Anton Yelchin") and [Irène Jacob](/wiki/Ir%C3%A8ne_Jacob "Irène Jacob"). In post-production Schrader was denied final cut by the film's producers.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The film was negatively received by many film critics and was a [box-office bomb](/wiki/Box-office_bomb "Box-office bomb"). Schrader later recut *Dying of the Light* into the separate, more experimental work *Dark*, which received more positive reviews.

### 2017–present: Career resurgence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Schrader&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2017–present: Career resurgence")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Paul_Schrader_Venice_2022.jpg/250px-Paul_Schrader_Venice_2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paul_Schrader_Venice_2022.jpg)

Schrader at the [2022 Venice Film Festival](/wiki/2022_Venice_Film_Festival "2022 Venice Film Festival")

Schrader's dramatic thriller *[First Reformed](/wiki/First_Reformed "First Reformed")*, starring [Ethan Hawke](/wiki/Ethan_Hawke "Ethan Hawke"), premiered at the 2017 [Venice Film Festival](/wiki/Venice_Film_Festival "Venice Film Festival") and received critical acclaim. Schrader received his first [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") nomination for the film in the category [Best Original Screenplay](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Original_Screenplay "Academy Award for Best Original Screenplay"). In 2021, he directed the crime drama film *[The Card Counter](/wiki/The_Card_Counter "The Card Counter")*, starring [Oscar Isaac](/wiki/Oscar_Isaac "Oscar Isaac") and [Tiffany Haddish](/wiki/Tiffany_Haddish "Tiffany Haddish"). The film also premiered at the 2021 Venice Film Festival and was widely lauded by critics. Schrader grouped these two films into a loose trilogy with another thriller, *[Master Gardener](/wiki/Master_Gardener_(film) "Master Gardener (film)")*, starring [Joel Edgerton](/wiki/Joel_Edgerton "Joel Edgerton") and [Sigourney Weaver](/wiki/Sigourney_Weaver "Sigourney Weaver"). Like the rest of the trilogy, it premiered at the Venice Film Festival in 2022, where Schrader was awarded the [Golden Lion Honorary Award](/wiki/Golden_Lion#Golden_Lion_Honorary_Award "Golden Lion").

In 2023, it was confirmed Schrader would write and direct *[Oh, Canada](/wiki/Oh,_Canada_(film) "Oh, Canada (film)")*, an adaptation of his friend [Russell Banks](/wiki/Russell_Banks "Russell Banks")' novel, *Foregone*, starring [Richard Gere](/wiki/Richard_Gere "Richard Gere") and [Jacob Elordi](/wiki/Jacob_Elordi "Jacob Elordi").[[16]](#cite_note-indiewire1234904461-16) Additionally, Schrader has written a western called *Three Guns at Dawn,* for [Antoine Fuqua](/wiki/Antoine_Fuqua "Antoine Fuqua") to direct; a drama about a trauma nurse called *R.N* for [Elisabeth Moss](/wiki/Elisabeth_Moss "Elisabeth Moss") to star in and direct; and a script about a sex addict called *Non Compos Mentis* which he intended to direct.[[17]](#cite_note-FilmStage-17)[[18]](#cite_note-Mentis2-18)

## Theatre career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Schrader&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Theatre career")]

Schrader has written two stage plays, *Berlinale* and *Cleopatra Club*. The latter saw its premiere at the [Powerhouse Theater](/wiki/Powerhouse_Theater "Powerhouse Theater") in [Poughkeepsie](/wiki/Poughkeepsie_(town),_New_York "Poughkeepsie (town), New York"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), in 1995 and its foreign language debut in [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna") in 2011.[[8]](#cite_note-sos-8)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Themes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Schrader&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Themes")]

A recurring theme in Schrader's films is the protagonist on a self-destructive path, or undertaking actions which work against himself, deliberately or subconsciously. The finale often bears an element of redemption, preceded by a painful sacrifice or cathartic act of violence.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Schrader has repeatedly referred to *Taxi Driver*, *American Gigolo*, *Light Sleeper*, *The Canyons*, *The Walker*, *First Reformed*, and *The Card Counter* as "a man in a room" stories. The protagonist in each film changes from an angry, then narcissistic, later anxious character, to a person who hides behind a mask of superficiality.[[8]](#cite_note-sos-8)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Although many of his films or scripts are based on real-life biographies *(Raging Bull*, *Mishima: A Life in Four Chapters*, *Patty Hearst*, *Auto Focus)*, Schrader confessed having problems with biographical films due to their altering of actual events, which he tried to prevent by imposing structures and stylization.[[8]](#cite_note-sos-8)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Schrader&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Personal life")]

### Marriage and health issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Schrader&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Marriage and health issues")]

Schrader battled a cocaine addiction, which contributed to his divorce from his first wife, art director [Jeannine Oppewall](/wiki/Jeannine_Oppewall "Jeannine Oppewall"). He then moved from Los Angeles to Japan in hopes of getting his life on track, finally quitting drugs around 1990. His second marriage is to actress [Mary Beth Hurt](/wiki/Mary_Beth_Hurt "Mary Beth Hurt"), who has appeared in smaller roles in a variety of his films.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Together they have two children, a daughter and a son.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In September 2022, Schrader was hospitalized for "breathing problems".[[25]](#cite_note-25) In January 2023, he and his wife moved from New York's suburban [Putnam County](/wiki/Putnam_County,_New_York "Putnam County, New York") to a luxury [assisted-living facility](/wiki/Assisted_living_facility "Assisted living facility") in Manhattan's [Hudson Yards](/wiki/Hudson_Yards,_Manhattan "Hudson Yards, Manhattan") area, where Hurt receives treatment for her [Alzheimer's](/wiki/Alzheimer%27s "Alzheimer's").[[26]](#cite_note-Quinlan-26)

### Views and beliefs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Schrader&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Views and beliefs")]

Schrader is a [Christian](/wiki/Christian "Christian"). Raised [Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinist "Calvinist"), Schrader abandoned religion in his young adulthood, before returning to Christianity later in life. He became an [Episcopalian](/wiki/Episcopalian "Episcopalian") after the birth of his children. As of 2018, he attends a [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian") church.[[27]](#cite_note-27) His films frequently feature religious themes.[[28]](#cite_note-28) However, Schrader has now emphasized that he considers himself to be just a Christian.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

In December 2016, Schrader referred to the then-upcoming [Trump presidency](/wiki/First_presidency_of_Donald_Trump "First presidency of Donald Trump") as "a call to violence" and said "we should be willing to take arms. Like Old [John Brown](/wiki/John_Brown_(abolitionist) "John Brown (abolitionist)")." He quickly deleted the post, but was visited by the [New York City Police Department Counterterrorism Bureau](/wiki/New_York_City_Police_Department_Counterterrorism_Bureau "New York City Police Department Counterterrorism Bureau") for threatening violence. Schrader expressed some regret for his post (blaming it on him drinking alcohol and taking an [Ambien](/wiki/Zolpidem "Zolpidem")), apologizing for his post's violent rhetoric, but not for his comments critical of Trump.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

In 2021, Schrader attacked [cancel culture](/wiki/Cancel_culture "Cancel culture"), describing it as "infectious...like the [Delta virus](/wiki/SARS-CoV-2_Delta_variant "SARS-CoV-2 Delta variant")".[[31]](#cite_note-31) In 2022, Schrader criticized that year's [*Sight and Sound* Greatest Films poll](/wiki/The_Sight_and_Sound_Greatest_Films_of_All_Time_2022 "The Sight and Sound Greatest Films of All Time 2022"), describing it as a "politically correct rejiggering", with its selection of *[Jeanne Dielman, 23 quai du Commerce, 1080 Bruxelles](/wiki/Jeanne_Dielman,_23_quai_du_Commerce,_1080_Bruxelles "Jeanne Dielman, 23 quai du Commerce, 1080 Bruxelles")* as the greatest film of all time being the product of "distorted woke reappraisal".[[32]](#cite_note-Jeanne-32) In 2023, he also criticized the perceived politicization of the [95th Academy Awards](/wiki/95th_Academy_Awards "95th Academy Awards"), writing that the Oscars' "scramble to be woke" have made their ceremony "mean less each year".[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Allegations of sexual harassment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Schrader&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Allegations of sexual harassment")]

In 2025 civil court documents made public allegations of [sexual harassment](/wiki/Sexual_harassment "Sexual harassment") and [sexual assault](/wiki/Sexual_assault "Sexual assault") made against Schrader by an unidentified woman who was his assistant. The alleged incidents took place between 2021 and 2024. Schrader reportedly had agreed to a confidential settlement with his accuser but ultimately reneged. Schrader’s attorney, Phillip Kessler, responded that there were two kisses during nights of drinking — one at the [Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/Cannes_Film_Festival "Cannes Film Festival") in May 2024 and another some months prior — but that Schrader backed down after his advances were unreciprocated.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)
